era_context: The Abbasid Caliphate experiences a succession crisis as al-Ma'mun dies and is succeeded by al-Mu'tasim. The Carolingian Empire is in the midst of civil war over succession. The Tang Dynasty continues its gradual decline. Viking raids on Western Europe are intensifying. In the Islamic world, the Mu'tazilite theological movement, patronised by al-Ma'mun, faces challenges under the new caliph.

events.title: Death of Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun; succession of al-Mu'tasim
events.description: Al-Ma'mun, the seventh Abbasid caliph (r. 813–833), dies in August 833 after a reign marked by military campaigns against the Byzantine Empire, the consolidation of Abbasid power, and the patronage of Mu'tazilite theology and the House of Wisdom. He is succeeded by his brother al-Mu'tasim (r. 833–842), who will pursue a different political and military strategy, emphasising the recruitment of Turkish slave-soldiers and a more aggressive stance toward Byzantine territories. The succession is smooth, but it marks a shift in the character of Abbasid rule.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, 9th–10th century

events.certainty_note: Al-Ma'mun's death in 833 is well-attested in Islamic historiography. The date is secure.
cross_references: Marks transition in Abbasid policy and military strategy

events.title: Carolingian civil war: Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye; Louis the Pious defeated by his sons
events.description: In June 833, the aging Carolingian emperor Louis the Pious is defeated in battle by a coalition of his own sons—Lothair I, Louis the German, and Charles the Bald—at Fontenoy-en-Puisaye (also called the Battle of Fontenay). This decisive military defeat marks a major turning point in the Carolingian succession crisis. Louis is forced to abdicate temporarily, and his sons move to partition the empire. The battle is one of the largest and bloodiest of the Carolingian period, with contemporary sources reporting enormous casualties.
key_figures: Louis the German (future king of East Francia)
Charles the Bald (future king of West Francia)

sources.name: Astronomer, Life of Louis the Pious, 9th century

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye in 833 is extensively documented in Frankish chronicles. The date and general course of the battle are secure, though casualty figures may be exaggerated.
cross_references: Precedes Treaty of Verdun (843), which formalises the partition of the empire

events.title: Viking raid on Lindisfarne and other English monasteries; escalation of Norse attacks
events.description: Viking raids on English monasteries, including Lindisfarne, intensify in 833. These raids are part of the broader pattern of Norse expansion into Western Europe during the 830s. The targeting of monasteries is partly due to their wealth and relative lack of fortification, and partly due to the religious motivations of pagan Norse raiders.

sources.name: Asser, Life of King Alfred, 9th century

events.certainty_note: Viking activity in 833 is attested in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, but the specific monasteries targeted and the scale of raids may be conflated or misdated in later compilation.
cross_references: Part of broader Viking Age expansion in 9th century

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Wenzong's reign continues amid factional conflict; literati-eunuch tensions escalate
events.description: Emperor Wenzong of Tang (r. 827–840) faces intensifying factional conflict between eunuchs and scholar-officials (literati) within the imperial court. The year 833 sees continued political instability as these factions compete for influence over imperial policy. This internal conflict contributes to the broader decline of Tang central authority and will eventually lead to the Huichang Persecution of Buddhism (841–847).

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang, 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tangshu), 10th century

events.certainty_note: The factional conflicts of Wenzong's reign are well-documented in later Chinese historiography, but precise dating of individual episodes to 833 is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye is sometimes presented as a clear-cut victory for the sons over Louis the Pious; however, some modern scholars argue that the political situation was more complex, and that Louis retained significant support even after the battle. The casualty figures reported in contemporary sources (some claiming 100,000+ deaths) are almost certainly exaggerated. Regarding al-Ma'mun's death, some sources give slightly different dates (late 833 vs. early 834), though August 833 is the scholarly consensus.
historiographic_note: The year 833 is well-documented in Frankish and Islamic sources, though both are filtered through later compilation and court perspective. The Frankish sources on the civil war are relatively abundant but often reflect the perspective of the victorious sons. Islamic sources on al-Ma'mun's death and al-Mu'tasim's succession are generally reliable but written decades or centuries after the events. Chinese sources on the Tang Dynasty are abundant but reflect the perspective of the literati class. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from contemporary written records.

graph_edges.note: Al-Mu'tasim's succession leads to establishment of Samarra as new capital
Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye leads to Treaty of Verdun partition of Carolingian Empire
Escalating Viking raids of 830s lead to Great Heathen Army invasion of 865
Factional conflicts of 833 intensify, leading to Huichang Persecution of Buddhism (841–847)
